questions.description: Fictitious Directive 2021/777 of the European Parliament and of the… Directive, Mortgage.
questions.text: Fictitious Directive 2021/777 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 31 October 2021 on the harmonisation of gambling services includes amongst its main provisions:- - Article 3, which requires setting a total maximum betting daily limit of €500 per person, applicable in all EU Member States for both in-shop and online gambling; and - Article 4, which obliges EU Member States to charge a levy of 10% on all individual bets over €200 (as a form of deterrent on gamblers placing excessive stakes). These provisions were to be transposed into the national laws of all EU Member States by 31st January 2023. The Oireachtas has amended several pieces of domestic legislation, including the (fictitious) Gaming and Lotteries Act 1986, the Gambling Control Act 2012 and the Betting Act 1990 to reflect the new requirements set by Fictitious Directive 2021/777 on foot of Government-proposed bills. However, given the complexity of current Irish law on gambling, none of these amendments will come into effect until January 31st 2025. On May 12th 2023, Billy went into his local privately-owned betting shop and placed three separate bets of €300 each on three greyhound races. No levy was charged. He also staked another €225 with a Maltese-registered online gambling website on the outcome of a football match. He won on none of these bets. Over the following week he gambled another €4,500 to try and recoup his losses. Only one bet came good, giving him winnings of €250 and a net loss for that week of €4,250. Billy's gambling in May 2023 has used up all of his savings and placed him in debt. He cannot now afford to pay the mortgage on his home and is threatened with house repossession. Billy has just found out about the existence of Directive 2021/777 on the harmonisation of gambling services. He comes to you as his solicitor for advice on whether he may have any grounds under EU law on: (i) direct effect; and/or (ii) state liability for taking an action against either the betting shops and/or Ireland, to try and recoup his gambling losses. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law throughout, advise Billy accordingly.

questions.description: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has…
questions.text: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has changed within the EU legal order since its foundation in the 1950s and how the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights has, since it became legally binding, now become the main basis upon which the CJEU ensures that human rights are observed in the Union legal order.

questions.description: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European…
questions.text: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European Parliament in the legislative process that should satisfactorily address any outstanding concerns that may have existed before the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon about a lack of democratic decision-making in the creation European Union laws?

questions.description: On 25 January 2020, Carlos, an Argentinian national, married Carmen, a…
questions.text: On 25 January 2020, Carlos, an Argentinian national, married Carmen, a Spanish national. Carmen has never exercised her right to move freely within the European Union --- she has always lived and worked in Spain (mostly part-time on minimum wage). Carlos and Carmen have a daughter, Camila, who is a Spanish national. Camila was born in Spain on 15 March 2014. Carlos has made an application for a residence permit in Spain on the basis that he is a family member of a Union citizen. Carlos has had his application for the residence permit refused by the Spanish authorities. It was refused on two grounds: (1) that Carlos has a criminal record in Spain (he has a criminal conviction for driving under the influence of drugs; another conviction for driving without a licence; and a third conviction for driving without any insurance --- these convictions all resulted from the same incident when Carlos was pulled over by police while driving erratically, although he had not crashed the car nor had he injured himself or any other person); and (2) that Carmen does not have sufficient financial resources to support herself and her family members. Carlos has now appealed the decision to refuse to grant him a residency permit on the basis that he, Carmen and Camila could be forced to leave the European Union because of the relationship of dependency between them. Referring to relevant provisions of the TFEU, EU secondary legislation and CJEU case law, advise Carlos on the strength of his appeal against the decision to refuse to grant him a residency permit in Spain as the family member of Union citizen(s).

questions.description: The Minister for Health in Ireland has recently introduced a (fictitious)… Regulation.
questions.text: The Minister for Health in Ireland has recently introduced a (fictitious) set of regulations designed to help combat the significant detrimental health implications caused by the hazardous consumption of alcohol. Included in these (fictitious) Public Health (Alcohol Consumption Reduction) Regulations 2024 is a requirement that all beer sold in supermarkets and shops in Ireland must have a maximum alcohol content of 4% by volume. Beer sold in pubs and restaurants can have an alcohol content by volume above 4%. The Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd., based in Westport, County Mayo, produces a very popular pale ale, with an alcohol by volume content of 6% and a very popular lager, with an alcohol by volume content of 7%. Both products are only sold in shops and are not available in pubs or restaurants. The Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd. currently only supply their products to the Irish market. Many Irish breweries have already indicated that they may be willing to alter or reformulate their products for shop and supermarket sale, reducing the alcohol content of their products to 4% by volume or below. The Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd. maintain that reducing the alcohol content of their most popular beers, given that these have a relatively high alcohol content, would remove some of their main characteristics and that the modified product would not appeal to as many consumers. The Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd. have come to you for advice on whether these new (fictitious) Public Health (Alcohol Consumption Reduction) Regulations 2024, which are due to come into effect in February 2025, are compatible with EU law on the free movement of goods. Referring to CJEU case-law on Articles 34 TFEU and 36 TFEU throughout, advise the Wild Atlantic Brewery Ltd. On the compatibility or otherwise of the 2024 Irish Regulations with EU law.

questions.description: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with…
questions.text: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts; and (ii) judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2024 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR 1-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the case-law of the Union judicature interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each casenote: (a) the core…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each casenote: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: (i) Case C-438/05, International Transport Workers Federation and Finnish Seamen's Union v Viking Line ABP and OCI Viking Line Eesti [2007] ECR 1-10779; (ii) Case C-384/93, Alpine Investments BV v Minister van Financién [1995] ECR I-1141; (iii) Case 140/79, Chemial Farmaceutici v DAF SpA [1981] ECR 1; (iv) Cases T-778/16, Ireland v Commission and T-892/16, Apple Sales International and Apple Operations Europe v Commission (v) Case 46/76, Bauhuis v Netherlands [1977] ECR 5; (vi) Case 106/77, Amministrazione delle Finanze dello Stato v Simmenthal SpA [1978] ECR 629; (vii) Case 262/88, Barber v Guardian Royal Exchange Assurance Group [1990] ECR 1889; (viii) Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Siochäna v Workplace Relations Commission ECLI:EU:C:2018:979.

questions.description: Explain, by reference to relevant CJEU case-law, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in…
questions.text: Explain, by reference to relevant CJEU case-law, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101 (1) TFEU. Also explain, by reference to relevant CJEU case-law, how a "dominant position" can be held by "one or more undertakings" under Article 102 TFEU.
